summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's unusual announcement of a 'Scary Fast' event scheduled for Monday evening, October 30, speculating about what products will be revealed. He considers whether the iMac will get an M2 or M3 chip, weighing TSMC's 3nm production constraints and the awkwardness of launching M3 Macs before the M2-based Vision Pro ships. He explores multiple scenarios, from a simple M2 iMac refresh to a more ambitious launch of M2 Pro/Max iMac Pros. His postscript theory attempts to reconcile all the clues: M2 iMacs with color-matched cables plus new pro-level iMacs with M2 Pro/Max chips, which would justify the 'Scary Fast' branding without requiring the M3.
key_points: The evening broadcast time (8pm ET / 5pm PT) is unprecedented for Apple events, suggesting something unusual about the format or content
TSMC's 3nm production is likely almost entirely dedicated to A17 Pro chips for iPhones 15 Pro, making M3 Mac launches premature
Launching M3-based Macs before the M2-powered Vision Pro ships would create an awkward product hierarchy
The 'Scary Fast' branding suggests something more impressive than a simple M2 speed bump — possibly M2 Pro/Max iMacs
Despite extensive analyst predictions, no one seems certain what exactly Apple will announce, which Gruber finds enjoyable
money_quotes: Broadcasting an event at 8pm ET / 5pm PT is new, but we're still relatively early in the era of pre-recorded keynotes.
It doesn't feel right to me that the M3 chip would debut in the iMac.
It would sort of feel awkward to start launching $1,300 M3-based iMacs months ahead of the $3,500 Vision Pro, which will have the M2.
It feels natural to me that M3-based Macs and iPads will only launch after the Vision Pro, so that when the Vision Pro is announced, the M2 is still the 'current' Apple silicon generation.
It's fun that no one seems to know what exactly is coming.
M2 Pro and Max iMacs would justify 'Scary Fast' in a way that just plain M2 iMac speed bumps would not.
key_insight: Apple's product launch timing is constrained not just by chip readiness but by the need to maintain a coherent product hierarchy, particularly avoiding the awkwardness of shipping M3 Macs before the M2-based Vision Pro arrives.
